coredata.dc:title: 3D Ag/NiCo-layered double hydroxide with adsorptive and photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Liu, Xiqing
Ma, Changchang
Liu, Yang
Dong, Hongjun
Ma, Wei
Liu, Zhi
Wei, Maobin
Li, Chunxiang
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a 3D-Ag/NiCo-LDHs photocatalyst was successfully prepared. Varies analytical techniques demonstrated that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs showed excellent photocatalytic activity than pure NiCo-LDHs, which may attribute the special structure of NiCo-LDHs with adsorption performance and surface plasma resonance effect (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles. Such flower-like structure of NiCo-LDHs, the petals staggered to form a plurality of independent reaction spaces to provide more reaction space and avoid the interaction between the reaction products. Meanwhile, combining the carbon quantum dots with the SPR of Ag nanoparticles can further enhance the photocatalytic activity. Based on the analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism was presented.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D NiCo-LDHs
Ag nanoparticles
Surface plasma resonance
Adsorption performance
Photocatalytic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images (a), TEM images (b, inset) and HRTEM images (c) of Ag/NiCo-LDHs. The EDS mapping of Ni (d), Ag (e) and Co (f).
XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR patterns (b) of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of Ag (a), Ni (b) and Co (c) of Ag/NiCo-LDHs and NiCo-LDHs. The N2 adsorption-desorption curves (d) of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs.
UV–vis absorption (a) and PL spectra curves (b) of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs.
Photocurrent response (a), ESI (b) of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs.

Adsorption capacities as a function of contact time for NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs (a, inset). Photocatalytic degradation in the present of as-prepared samples (b). Kinetic fit for the degradation of different samples (c). The cycle runs of the TC removal by the effects of absorption-photocatalysis of Ag/NiCo-LDHs (d).
Valence-band XPS spectra (a) and trapping experiment of Ag/NiCo-LDHs.
Scheme diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism of Ag/NiCo-LDH under visible illumination.
Kinetic constants for the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models.

head.ce:title: 3D Ag/NiCo-layered double hydroxide with adsorptive and photocatalytic performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a 3D-Ag/NiCo-LDHs photocatalyst was successfully prepared. Varies analytical techniques demonstrated that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs showed excellent photocatalytic activity than pure NiCo-LDHs, which may attribute the special structure of NiCo-LDHs with adsorption performance and surface plasma resonance effect (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles. Such flower-like structure of NiCo-LDHs, the petals staggered to form a plurality of independent reaction spaces to provide more reaction space and avoid the interaction between the reaction products. Meanwhile, combining the carbon quantum dots with the SPR of Ag nanoparticles can further enhance the photocatalytic activity. Based on the analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism was presented.

A 3D flower-like Ag/NiCo-layered double hydroxide material with high photocatalytic activity was achieved for adsorption and plasma resonance effect of removing tetracycline.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the great advances achieved in nanomaterials fabrication, generation by simple process remains a necessary task in order to widen the applications [1,2]. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), as a family of layered anionic materials, have caused great attention due to their great stability, low cost, flexible structures, and chemical versatility, which have been exploited as a prosperous source of materials for application in catalysis, electrochemistry, and chemical sensing [3,4]. Especially, LDHs have been broadly used as heterogeneous catalysts for base-catalyzed reactions or redox transformations, but the photocatalytic properties of LDHs have been generally ignored. García et al. first reported the high-induced oxygen generation, some efficient LDHs-based photocatalysts were later be found, such as NiTi-LDHs, ZnCr-LDHs/graphene, and ZnCr-LDHs/layered titanate [5–8]. Parida and coworkers reported that the carbonate intercalated ZnCr-LDHs and ternary Mg (Al + Fe)- and (Ni + Zn) Cr-LDHs have good photocatalytic activity towards hydrogen evolution [9–11]. All the same, there was only a few report about photocatalytic degradation for contamination. Therefore, it is significant to explore the photocatalytic activity of LDHs in the field of environmental governance. In particular, NiCo-LDHs derived from Ni and Co hydroxides have high redox activity (providing multiple oxidation states), abundant sources and environmental friendliness [12,13]. However, it is still a challenge to overcome the bad recurrence and a high recombination of electron–hole pairs.

In recent years, an easier way to extend the light absorption range and improve the rates of recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes in photocatalyst is to dope it with a transition metal or noble metal ions. Ag-doped TiO2 microcrystals have been reported to exhibit photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation [14]. Surface loading of a number of metallic Ag nanoparticles on some photocatalysts as well as doping Ag ions into the phase structure have led to an enhancement photocatalytic activity. For example, Fageria et al. demonstrated that the photocatalytic activity of Ag deposition on ZnO surface for the degradation methylene blue [15]. Additionally, the photonic efficiency of all metal-doped photocatalyst (i.e., Pt, Au, and Pd) was found to be higher than the pure due to the acceleration of hydroxyl radical construction and decline recombination. Jiang et al. used Ag/Pt loaded TiO2 as a catalyst in degradation of rhodamine B/ciprofloxacin under visible light illumination and exhibited highly photocatalytic activity [16]. These results indicated that the load of Ag reduces the rate of charge carrier recombination and improves photocatalytic activity. However, the effect of Ag modification on the photocatalytic performance of NiCo-LDHs has not been explored extensively.

Therefore, the aim of this study was to assess the photocatalytic activity of NiCo-LDHs by additional loading with metal Ag. In this work, a series of Ag nanoparticles modified NiCo-LDHs photocatalysts were prepared. Meanwhile, the influences of the Ag contents on the surficial properties and optical absorption of the photocatalysts were discussed. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline was investigated by using Ag/NiCo-LDHs, and the optimal Ag nanoparticles loading content was obtained. Additionally, the adsorption capacity was discussed due to the relatively high specific surface areas of samples. Finally, the promotion mechanisms of Ag/NiCo-LDHs were proposed.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2•6H2O), Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2•6H2O), isopropyl (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), Silver nitrate (AgNO3), tetracycline (TC) were obtained from Shanghai Reagents Company (Shanghai, China), and citric acid was obtained from Tianjin Chemical (Tianjin, China). All stock solutions were prepared daily with deionized water treated with a water purification system (Simplicity 185, Millipore Corp., Billerica, MA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiCo-LDHs flower-like and Ag/NiCo-LDHs
ce:section.ce:para: The NiCo-LDHs flower-like were fabricated by one-step chemical coprecipitation way [17]. Simply, Ni(NO3)2 (0.45 mmol), Co(NO3)2 (0.30 mmol), NH4Cl (4 mmol), and NaOH (2.06 mmol) were mixed in deionized water (20 mL) to obtain metal ammine mixture. After, the mixture was transferred into an oven for 15 h under 55 °C. Until it was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the as-prepared NiCo-LDHs sample was obtained by deionized water and ethanol washed for several times and dried.

Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites were prepared as follow. The NiCo-LDHs (0.20 g) were dipped in an aqueous solution of AgNO3 with proper contents after 30 min illumination under UV light (in the presence of atmospheric oxygen). After, the catalysts were thoroughly washed with ultrapure water and dried. The theoretical calculation of Ag mass ratio was 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%, respectively. The prepared photocatalysts were labeled as Ag/LDH-1, Ag/LDH-2, Ag/LDH-3, and Ag/LDH-4, respectively.

The adsorption experiments were conducted in dark. Briefly, an accurately weighed amount of catalysts (50 mg) were added into 100 mL TC solution with a magnetic stirring and maintained at 25 °C. The solution was periodically sampled for concentration determination by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Then, the adsorption capacity of catalysts for TC was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
q t
=
(
C 0
--
C t
)
V / m

Where qt is the adsorption capacity at time t (mg g−1, C 0 and Ct denote the TC concentration before and after the adsorption (mg L−1), V is the volume of TC solution (L) and m is the weight of catalysts used (g).

The photodegradation experiments were carried out under the irradiation of a 100 W iodine–tungsten lamp and maintained at 298 K after saturated adsorption. The photocatalysts (50 mg) were dispersed in 100 mL TC solution and stirred by a magnetic stirring. Then, 3 ml of the reaction solution was withdrawn by a syringe every 10 min for the determination of concentration.

X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of the samples recorded at room temperature, and the patterns of the photocatalyst were obtained with a D/max-RA X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) which equipped with Ni-filtrated Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 200 mA) at 10–80° with a scanning step of 7° min–1. The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Magna-IR 550 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer and KBr as the reference sample within the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were examined with JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (Nova Nano SEM 450, FEI). The room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated utilizing the (Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer, VARIAN, USA) equipped with xenon (Xe) lamp with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer by using BaSO4 as a reference. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured on a Cary 5000 (Agilent, USA) UV–vis spectrophotometer. The specific surface areas of the various materials were quantified using the Brunauer Emmett-Teller (BET) Micrometrics Tristar surface area. The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 760D (CH Instruments, Inc.) electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system with a platinum network counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode, using a concentration of 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte under ambient temperature (25 °C). The transient photocurrent response was performed on the electrochemical analyzer using 0.5 V bias voltage under light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp as the light source).

The typical SEM morphologies of the as-prepared Ag/NiCo-LDHs materials were shown in Fig. 1 (a), which suggested clearly that the products consist of uniform and high-quality microspheres. The detailed features of the products were shown in inset Fig. 1(a), which consisted of numerous sheets nearly perpendicular to the flower surface. In addition, the sheets were interconnected with each other and interlaced like petals, so they can provide more contact areas. This structures can provide more independent reaction space and the mechanism will be discussed in detail. However, it cannot find any Ag particles on the surface of petal which due to the content or size of Ag particles were too small [18,19].

To obtain more structural insight for the Ag/NiCo-LDHs materials, TEM and HRTEM results were also recorded. In Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs samples were flower-like and consist of sheets and form the integration of flower-like feature. This was in consist with the SEM image. Additionally, we can find a black dot on the surface, which may be the Ag nanoparticle. Details of the black dot area were given in Fig. 1(b) (inset image), which showed much Ag particles on the surface of the petal. HRTEM image in Fig. 1(c) clearly showed the resolved lattice fringes of 0.251 nm belong to the (004) crystal face of the Ag and 0.236 nm belong to the (110) crystal face of the hydrotalcite-like LDHs structure, respectively [20,21]. The result demonstrated that the Ag particles were nano-level and these Ag nanoparticles could promote the light absorption due to it SPR effect. The small size can benefit to provide more active sites to enhance the photocatalytic performance. The analysis was consistent with the results of UV–vis and photocatalytic experiments [22,23]. Moreover, Fig. 1(d) and (e) showed the elemental maps of Ag/NiCo-LDHs micro-flower, clearly displayed the homogeneous distribution of Ag, Ni and Co in the composite. It was known that the uniform distribution was an important factor in electrons transfer, which can effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity. The EDS pattern of Ag/NiCo-LDHs samples (Fig. S1), implied the presence of the elements Ni, Co, Ag, C and O. The content of Ag was 13.88%, which was closely to the theoretical value 15%. The above conclusions revealed that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs flower-like was successfully synthesized.

XRD was conducted to characterize the phase structure of the as-prepared materials [24]. Fig. 2 (a) showed the XRD pattern of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs. Both samples exhibited four characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 11°, 22°, 33° and 59°, which can be indexed to the (003), (006), (009) and (110) planes of hydrotalcite-like LDHs phase. The XRD pattern of Ag/NiCo-LDHs sample was similar to the bare NiCo-LDHs, indicating the existence of NiCo-LDH in the material. Compared to NiCo-LDHs, the XRD patterns of NiCo-LDHs after modification with Ag was similar, showed all peaks of NiCo-LDHs, suggesting that loaded of Ag nanoparticle cannot change the crystalline of NiCo-LDHs materials. But, it can be found that the intensity of the Ag/NiCo-LDHs flower was slightly lower than pure NiCo-LDHs, which may attribute to that Ag species deposit on the NiCo-LDHs.

To further demonstrate the as-prepared materials. Fig. 2(b) showed the FT-IR spectra of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs samples. The signal at 3460 cm−1 was the OH stretching band, arising from interlayer water molecules and metal-hydroxyl groups. The band centered at 1617 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibration of water. The band at 1386 cm−1 and 1355 cm−1 can be assigned to the vibration of interlayer CO3 2− and NO3 − anions. CO3 2- participated to form the nickel–cobalt carbonate hydroxide hydrate with Ni2+ and Co2+ ions by coordinate bonds while NO3 − retained in the interlayer of LDHs. The broad peak at 633 cm−1 can be assigned to the A–O, A–O–A, and O–A–O (A = Ni and Co) vibrations [25]. This results indicated that the materials were successfully be prepared and the Ag nanoparticles cannot affect the structure of NiCo-LDHs samples, which consisted of the result of XRD analysis.

The chemical composition and surface chemical state of the pure NiCo-LDHs and Ag/LDH-3 samples were investigated by XPS technique [26,27]. The XPS survey spectrum of the materials exhibited the peaks corresponding to Ag 3d, Ni 2p and Co 2p for Ag/LDH samples (Fig. S2), which was well consistent with the EDS mapping analysis. However, the peak of Ag was disappeared in the bare NiCo-LDHs. Furthermore, the peak of Ag 3d state at about 374.3 and 368.3 eV were obviously from the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ag-LDH (Fig. 3 (a)), which were attributed to Ag 3d5/2 (Ag0) and Ag 3d3/2 (Ag0), further confirming the existence of Ag0 [28]. In addition, the XPS spectra of Ni in bare NiCo-LDHs can be assigned to the binding energies of Ni 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 states at 873.4 and 855.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). The obvious binding energy peaks at 796.2 and 780.8 eV of Co3+ 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 states, respectively (Fig. 3(d)). Meanwhile, the other two fitting peaks at 797.3 and 782.1 eV were ascribed to Co2+. It was noted that the binding energies of Ni 2p state and Co 2p state in Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites were shift 0.6 and 0.5 eV toward higher binding energy compared with that of NiCo-LDHs, respectively, which should be closely related to Ag nanoparticles transfer electrons from photocatalysts.

BET was a parameter that assesses material performance and the larger specific surface area was conducive to the contact of target [29,30]. Fig. 3(d) showed N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs samples. Both isotherms were of type III and type H3 hysteresis loops at high P/P 0, which reflected the formation of pores from a combination of LDHs sheets. According to the BET equation computing results, the surface areas of LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs were 58.1 and 68.4 m2g−1, respectively. The surface area of Ag/NiCo-LDHs was increased by addition of Ag nanoparticle due to the particle size, which was consistent the HRTEM results [31,32]. The high surface area could promote the photocatalysis owe to the effect of adsorption and more active sites.

The use of light was an important evaluation factor for photocatalysts [33,34]. Fig. 4 (a) displayed the UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of the as-prepared samples. For the NiCo-LDHs, it has two absorption peaks at 600 and 640 nm, which due to d–d transitions of the Ni and Co ions. The band gap energies of the as-prepared samples were calculated by the formula based on the DRS results:
(2)
A h ν
=
A
( h ν −
E g
)
n / 2
where A, h, ν and Eg and are a constant, absorption coefficient, Plank constant, light frequency and band gap energy, respectively. According to the formula (2), n was determined from the type of optical transition from a semiconductor (n = 1 for direct transition and n = 4 for indirect transition). As depicted in Fig. 4(a), NiCo-LDHs owned a band gap of 2.7 eV. Compared with pure NiCo-LDHs, the Ag/NiCo-LDHs made the electronic absorption red-shifted due to intense surface plasmon resonance of Ag nanoparticles [35,36]. The expanded absorption would harvest more photons to produce more photogenerated charge carries, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity.

The efficient separation of electron–hole pairs can enhance the photocatalytic activity [37]. That PL spectrum was widely used to investigate the migration, transfer, and recombination of electron–hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 4(b), it can find that NiCo-LDHs displayed more intense fluorescence emission peak, while the PL intensity of Ag/NiCo-LDHs was further taking place more seriously quenching phenomenon. The lower PL intensity was linked with the excellent electrical conductivity and induced SPR effect [38,39]. Therefore, it suggested more effectively the separation of electron–hole pairs to improve photocatalytic activity.

The electrochemical behaviors of the as-prepared samples were investigated (Fig. 5 ). Photocurrent responses with good reproducibility were written down excitation of NiCo-LDHs and Ag/NiCo-LDHs with visible-light irradiation (Fig. 5(a)). The result was directly related to the electron transport and recombination efficiency of the photogenerated carriers [40,41]. The photocurrent density of the Ag/NiCo-LDHs was about twice that of bare NiCo-LDHs, which was agree with the function of the Ag nanoparticles in contributing to electron transport. Remarkably, the Ag/NiCo-LDHs exhibited an excellent enhanced photocurrent density, suggesting the composites benefit to transfer electrons due to Ag nanoparticles modified.

The charge transfer resistance and separation competence of the charge carriers were further investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot of Ag/NiCo-LDHs was smaller than that of bare NiCo-LDHs, suggesting that the Ag loaded on the NiCo-LDHs surface substantially enhanced the separation and transfer available of electron–hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were also used to investigate the separation or recombination of photogenerated e−–h+ pairs of the photocatalysts (Fig. 4(b)). The result indicated that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites have a strong ability of the separation and transfer of electrons due to SPR effect [42,43].

The photodegradation of TC under visible-light irradiation have been chosen to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of NiCo-LDHs and a series of Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites. From the Fig. 6 (a), an obvious adsorption of TC was observed in all case in the dark. However, with the increased contents of Ag on the surface of NiCo-LDHs, the adsorption was essentially unchanged. It indicated that the adsorption capacity was mainly determined by NiCo-LDHs. In order to further determine the NiCo-LDHs played a role in adsorption, the adsorption mechanism was studied, and the obtained data were fitted by pseudo-first-order (Eq. (3)) and pseudo-second-order models (Eq. (4)), with the acquired kinetic parameters and correlation coefficients (R 2) listed in Table 1 . Comparing the R 2 value, the pseudo-second-order kinetic fit showed well agreement with experimental results. The fast adsorption velocity was probably attributed to the better affinity between NiCo-LDHs and TC molecules [44,45]. The kinetics of adsorption can be described as follows:
(3)
ln
(
q e
−
q t
)
= ln
q e
−
k 1
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
+
t
q e

Where qe (mg g−1) and qt (mg g−1) are the amounts of TC adsorption on NiCo-LDHs at equilibrium and at time t (min), respectively, and k 1 (g(mg min)−1) and k 2 (g(mg min)−1) are the pseudo-first-and pseudo-second-order rate constants, respectively [46,47].

In the case of all of the samples, about 50% TC molecules have been absorbed on the surface of materials after stirred in the dark for 40 min. Under the visible light irradiation, the only 20% of TC was degraded for pure NiCo-LDHs. The photocatalytic performances of Ag/NiCo-LDHs were influenced by the contents of Ag loading. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the degradation efficiency of TC by all Ag/NiCo-LDHs samples were higher than bare NiCo-LDHs. For Ag/LDH-3, the efficiency reaches over 90% after visible light irradiation. The optimum of Ag nanoparticles could improve the active sites and access the electron transfer. But increasing the Ag content, the photocatalytic activity was decreased due to the high coverage of the Ag nanoparticles reduced the access of light to the NiCo-LDHs surface to produce photogenerated electron-hole pairs [48,49]. Although the degradation ration may be influenced by the initial adsorption, the kinetic linear simulation curves can vividly reveal the photocatalytic activity of these composites [50]. Using the initial concentration of the TC after adsorption equilibrium as C0, the calculated curves of k for the photocatalysts and the rate constants (k) were listed in Table 2 . It can be seen that the Ag/LDH-3 samples have the maximum rate constant of 0.021 min−1, which was about 6 times higher than bare NiCo-LDHs. The compounds degradation rate was higher than the monomer, which may be attributed to the Ag nanoparticles on the surface of the NiCo-LDHs could accept the photogenerated electrons and prolong the lifetime of charge carriers due to the surface plasmon resonance effect.

For materials, it should be also easily regenerated for the recyclable application. Fig. 6(d) showed cycle runs of TC degradation on Ag/LDH-3. The TC adsorption capacity slight decreased after 5 cycles and the TC removal effectively was still over 85% due to the adsorption-photocatalysis synergistic effect. The synergy between adsorption and photocatalysis has outstanding efficiency than pre-adsorption. This results indicated that the materials have excellently stability and repeatability.

To further evaluate what kind of radical plays the main role in the degradation process, reactive species has been investigated by adding different trapping agents [51,52]. The experiments were conducted under the same conditions except for adding different radical scavengers. The scavenging of O2
.
− was captured by N2 purging, whereas IPA and TEOA have been used as •OH and h+ scavengers, respectively (Fig. 7 (b)). The photodegradation of TC showed a marked decreases in presence of TEOA were the main reactive species in this case. To explain this observation, the photocatalytic mechanism of this system was proposed. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) position Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites could be estimated by the following equations:
(5)
E
V B
=
x
−
E e
+
0.5
E g
(6)
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g

Where EVB and ECB were the VB and CB edge, x was the absolute electronegativity of semiconductors, Ee was the potential energy of free electrons in the standard hydrogen electron, E g was the band gap energy of the materials. Based on the above equations, the EVB can test to be 1.6 eV (Fig. 7(a)) and E CB was calculated to be −1.0 eV, respectively. Since the E VB was more negative than E
•
OH/H2O (+ 2.68 eV vs. NHE), the trapped holes in the value band cannot oxidize H2O to yield •OH. The E CB was more positive than E O2/O2
.
- (–0.046 eV vs. NHE), the electrons in the conduction band can reduce O2 to yield O2
.
−. Hence, the photodegradation of TC was carried out predominantly by h+ and O2
.
−, which verified the results of trapping experiments.

Based on the scavenger analysis, the increased photocatalytic mechanism of Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites under light irradiation was being displayed in Fig. 8 . The enhanced photocatalytic performance might be attributed to the formation of Schottky barrier at the interface between Ag and NiCo-LDHs, which led to fast transfer of electrons from the CB of NiCo-LDHs to the Ag. Such situation could prolong the lifetime of electron–hole pairs, hence enhancing the photocatalytic performance of Ag/NiCo-LDHs composites. Therefore, the Ag nanoparticles can act as acceptor of photoelectrons to prolong the lifetime of electron–hole pairs [28]. The photogenerated electrons on the surface of Ag nanoparticles were trapped by O2 molecules that were consequently transferred into O2 .−. At the same time, holes generated in the NiCo-LDHs will react with TC. Furthermore, the SPR induced by the collective oscillations of the surface electrons on Ag nanoparticles could increase the light catching of photocatalyst and enhance the efficiency of electron–hole pairs. In this case, TC molecules can be subsequently degraded into mineralized products by the action O2 .− and h+ radicals. The whole photocatalytic process can be described as follows.
(1) NiCo-LDHs + hv –––––→NiCo-LDHs (e−) + (h+)
(2) NiCo-LDHs (e−) + Ag–––––→NiCo-LDHs (e−) + Ag (e−)
(3) Ag (e−) + O2–––––→O2 .− + Ag
(4) O2 .− + h+ + TC–––––→Mineralized products

In this work, the 3D structure of Ag/NiCo-LDHs were consisted of many sheets, and these sheets were staggered to form many separate spaces, which can be demonstrated by SEM images. Based on the XPS, EDS mapping and HRTEM, we can demonstrate that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs were prepared successfully. The special structure and adsorption performance of composite can provide a better environment for photocatalytic reaction. Meanwhile, the Ag nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of NiCo-LDHs further enhanced the photocatalytic activity, which due to the surface plasma resonance effect (SPR) and small size of Ag nanoparticles. In addition, the spectra of UV–vis, PL, photocurrent, and EIS were demonstrated that the Ag/NiCo-LDHs showed higher electron separation efficiency. The capture the experiment was proved that the O2
.
− and h+ were the main active species. Such special structure would have wide application in photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21676115, U1510126, U1662125), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Provincial (BK20171313, BK20150536), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M590418, 2015M571683, 2017M611732, 2017M611716) and Innovation Programs Foundation of Jiangsu Province (KYLX16_0877).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2018.07.031.
